theorem_title: Weak solution equals unique minimizer of the Dirichlet energy
theorem_statement:
Weak solution equals unique minimizer of the Dirichlet energy.

Let \(\Omega \subset \mathbb{R}^d\) be a bounded domain with sufficiently regular boundary, and let \(f \in L^2(\Omega)\). Define
\[
J(v) := \frac12 \int_\Omega |\nabla v|^2 \, dx - \int_\Omega f v \, dx
\qquad \text{for } v \in H_0^1(\Omega).
\]

Then \(u \in H_0^1(\Omega)\) satisfies
\[
\int_\Omega \nabla u \cdot \nabla \varphi \, dx
=
\int_\Omega f \varphi \, dx
\qquad \text{for every } \varphi \in H_0^1(\Omega)
\]
if and only if \(u\) is the unique minimizer of \(J\) over \(H_0^1(\Omega)\).
raw_proof_text:
We prove both directions.

First suppose that \(u \in H_0^1(\Omega)\) is a weak solution, meaning that for every \(\varphi \in H_0^1(\Omega)\),
\[
\int_\Omega \nabla u \cdot \nabla \varphi \, dx
=
\int_\Omega f \varphi \, dx.
\]

We want to show that \(u\) minimizes the functional
\[
J(v)=\frac12\int_\Omega |\nabla v|^2\,dx-\int_\Omega f v\,dx
\]
over all \(v \in H_0^1(\Omega)\), and that this minimizer is unique.

So let \(v \in H_0^1(\Omega)\) be arbitrary. Write
\[
w := v-u.
\]
Then \(w \in H_0^1(\Omega)\) as well, and
\[
v = u+w.
\]

Now compute \(J(v)\). We have
\[
J(v)
=
\frac12\int_\Omega |\nabla(u+w)|^2\,dx
-
\int_\Omega f(u+w)\,dx.
\]
Expand the square:
\[
|\nabla(u+w)|^2
=
|\nabla u|^2 + 2 \nabla u \cdot \nabla w + |\nabla w|^2.
\]
Substituting this into the functional gives
\[
J(v)
=
\frac12\int_\Omega |\nabla u|^2\,dx
+
\int_\Omega \nabla u \cdot \nabla w\,dx
+
\frac12\int_\Omega |\nabla w|^2\,dx
-
\int_\Omega f u\,dx
-
\int_\Omega f w\,dx.
\]

Group the terms corresponding to \(J(u)\):
\[
J(v)
=
\left(
\frac12\int_\Omega |\nabla u|^2\,dx - \int_\Omega f u\,dx
\right)
+
\left(
\int_\Omega \nabla u \cdot \nabla w\,dx - \int_\Omega f w\,dx
\right)
+
\frac12\int_\Omega |\nabla w|^2\,dx.
\]
That is,
\[
J(v)
=
J(u)
+
\left(
\int_\Omega \nabla u \cdot \nabla w\,dx - \int_\Omega f w\,dx
\right)
+
\frac12\int_\Omega |\nabla(v-u)|^2\,dx.
\]

Now use the weak equation with test function \(\varphi = w\). Since \(w \in H_0^1(\Omega)\), the weak formulation gives
\[
\int_\Omega \nabla u \cdot \nabla w\,dx
=
\int_\Omega f w\,dx.
\]
Therefore the middle bracket vanishes. Hence
\[
J(v)
=
J(u)
+
\frac12\int_\Omega |\nabla(v-u)|^2\,dx.
\]

Since the integrand \(|\nabla(v-u)|^2\) is nonnegative, we obtain
\[
J(v)\ge J(u).
\]
So \(u\) is a minimizer of \(J\).

Now we show uniqueness. If \(v \neq u\), then \(v-u\) is not the zero element of \(H_0^1(\Omega)\). In the \(H_0^1\) setting, this means its gradient is not zero in \(L^2\), so
\[
\int_\Omega |\nabla(v-u)|^2\,dx > 0.
\]
Therefore
\[
J(v)
=
J(u)
+
\frac12\int_\Omega |\nabla(v-u)|^2\,dx
>
J(u).
\]
So the minimizer is unique.

This proves the first direction: every weak solution is the unique minimizer.

Now conversely, suppose that \(u \in H_0^1(\Omega)\) is the unique minimizer of \(J\). We want to prove that \(u\) satisfies the weak equation
\[
\int_\Omega \nabla u \cdot \nabla \varphi \, dx
=
\int_\Omega f\varphi \, dx
\qquad \text{for every } \varphi \in H_0^1(\Omega).
\]

Let \(\varphi \in H_0^1(\Omega)\) be arbitrary. Consider the one-variable function
\[
g(t) := J(u+t\varphi), \qquad t \in \mathbb{R}.
\]
Because \(u\) minimizes \(J\), the point \(t=0\) is a minimum of \(g\). Therefore
\[
g'(0)=0.
\]

We now compute \(g'(0)\). By definition,
\[
g(t)
=
\frac12 \int_\Omega |\nabla(u+t\varphi)|^2\,dx
-
\int_\Omega f(u+t\varphi)\,dx.
\]
Expand the square:
\[
|\nabla(u+t\varphi)|^2
=
|\nabla u|^2 + 2t\,\nabla u \cdot \nabla \varphi + t^2 |\nabla \varphi|^2.
\]
Thus
\[
g(t)
=
\frac12\int_\Omega |\nabla u|^2\,dx
+
t\int_\Omega \nabla u \cdot \nabla \varphi\,dx
+
\frac{t^2}{2}\int_\Omega |\nabla \varphi|^2\,dx
-
\int_\Omega f u\,dx
-
t\int_\Omega f\varphi\,dx.
\]

Differentiate with respect to \(t\):
\[
g'(t)
=
\int_\Omega \nabla u \cdot \nabla \varphi\,dx
+
t\int_\Omega |\nabla \varphi|^2\,dx
-
\int_\Omega f\varphi\,dx.
\]
Evaluating at \(t=0\), we get
\[
g'(0)
=
\int_\Omega \nabla u \cdot \nabla \varphi\,dx
-
\int_\Omega f\varphi\,dx.
\]
But \(g'(0)=0\), so
\[
\int_\Omega \nabla u \cdot \nabla \varphi\,dx
=
\int_\Omega f\varphi\,dx.
\]
Since \(\varphi \in H_0^1(\Omega)\) was arbitrary, this is exactly the weak formulation.

Therefore \(u\) is a weak solution.

This proves the converse direction and completes the proof.
